title: Fitting a recessed LED downlight into a plasterboard ceiling
subtitle: Cut the hole, connect the flex, and clip in a fire-rated LED downlight
excerpt: How to fit a recessed LED downlight into a plasterboard ceiling. Covers choosing a fire-rated fitting, making the hole, connecting the flex to the loop-in junction box or lighting circuit, and clipping the fitting into the ceiling.
sourceNotes: BS 7671: Requirements for Electrical Installations (IET Wiring Regulations 18th Edition). Building Regulations Approved Document B (fire safety). NICEIC consumer guidance on downlights and fire safety. IET Guidance Note 7: Special Locations.

glossaryTerms.definition: A recessed downlight designed and tested to maintain the fire resistance of the ceiling in which it is fitted. Standard non-fire-rated fittings create a gap in the plasterboard when cut in, which can allow fire to spread between floors. Fire-rated fittings have an intumescent collar or a sealed housing that expands in heat to close the gap. Required by Building Regulations when a ceiling separates habitable floors (e.g., ground floor ceiling above a ground floor room).

The standard UK method of wiring a lighting circuit in which the circuit cable loops from fitting to fitting without a separate junction box. Each ceiling rose or downlight connection point has a cluster of connections: the mains (live, neutral, earth) looped from the previous fitting and the switch wire (live and neutral) going to the light switch. A recessed downlight replaces a ceiling rose and uses the same loop-in connection.

content.text: Recessed LED downlights are the most common ceiling light in modern UK homes. A single fitting replaces a pendant ceiling rose and requires making a circular hole in the plasterboard. The critical safety point is using a
wherever the ceiling separates two occupied floors or a floor and a roof void. The electrical connection uses the same

Isolate the lighting circuit at the consumer unit and confirm dead with the voltage tester before touching any conductors. Do not work on a live circuit. If you are not confident identifying the loop-in connections in the ceiling void, instruct a registered electrician.

A fire-rated LED downlight fitting with an integrated LED module or a GU10 lamp socket (check what the circuit wiring can accommodate). A hole saw or jab saw sized to the fitting's cut-out diameter (typically 70 to 75 mm). A cordless drill. A voltage tester. Wire strippers. Insulated terminal connectors (Wago lever connectors or similar). The electrician's screwdriver set.

Switch off the lighting circuit at the consumer unit. Test with the voltage tester at the existing light fitting or socket to confirm the circuit is dead. Leave a note at the consumer unit warning that the circuit is being worked on.
Step 2, mark and cut the hole

Mark the downlight position on the ceiling. Before cutting, check for joists above using a joist detector; the hole must not cut through a joist. Drill a small pilot hole first and probe upward with a stiff wire to check there is clear space in the ceiling void (no insulation blocking access, no cables directly over the hole). Cut the hole with the hole saw or jab saw to the diameter stated on the fitting instructions.
Step 3, connect the flex to the circuit

The fitting will have a short flex lead (or a flying lead to a heat-resistant connector block). Push the flex up through the hole and connect it to the loop-in connections in the ceiling void: live (brown) to the live conductors, neutral (blue) to the neutrals, earth (green/yellow) to the earth conductors. Use push-in Wago connectors rather than traditional choc-block connectors: Wacos are reversible, do not require tools, and are safer in a ceiling void. Ensure the fitting's earth is connected even if the fitting housing appears to be plastic.
Step 4, clip the fitting into the ceiling

Fold the flex lead and push it gently into the ceiling void above the hole. Push the downlight fitting into the hole; spring clips on the sides of the fitting splay outward against the back of the plasterboard and grip it. The fitting should sit flush with the ceiling face. Restore the circuit at the consumer unit and test the light. If the fitting has a separate driver (transformer), it must be accessible above the ceiling; do not bury it in insulation.

attrs.symptom: Light not working after fitting but circuit is live
attrs.cause: Loose connection at one of the Wago connectors; conductor not fully inserted.
attrs.fix: Isolate, pull the fitting down, and check each conductor is fully seated in its connector — press in firmly until you feel the lever click.

attrs.symptom: Fitting falls out of the ceiling
attrs.cause: Plasterboard is too thin for the spring clips, or the clips are not deployed.
attrs.fix: Check the fitting is rated for the plasterboard thickness. Some fittings require the spring clips to be manually deployed — squeeze and release while pushing the fitting up into the hole.
